papers.title: Seeding High Redshift QSOs by Collisional Runaway in Primordial Star Clusters
papers.author_names: Harley Katz Debora Sijacki Martin G. Haehnelt

abstract.text: We study how runaway stellar collisions in high-redshift, metal-poor star clusters form very massive stars (VMSs) that can directly collapse to intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs). We follow the evolution of a pair of neighbouring high-redshift mini-haloes with high-resolution, cosmological hydrodynamical zoom-in simulations using the adaptive mesh refinement code RAMSES combined with the non-equilibrium chemistry package KROME. The first collapsing mini-halo is assumed to enrich the central nuclear star cluster (NSC) of the other to a critical metallicity, sufficient for Population II (Pop. II) star formation at redshift $z\\\\approx27$. Using the spatial configuration of the flattened, asymmetrical gas cloud forming in the core of the metal enriched halo, we set the initial conditions for simulations of an initially non-spherical star cluster with the direct summation code NBODY6 which are compared to about 2000 NBODY6 simulations of spherical star clusters for a wide range of star cluster parameters. The final mass of the VMS that forms depends strongly on the initial mass and initial central density of the NSC. For the initial central densities suggested by our RAMSES simulations, VMSs with mass > 400 M$_{\\\\odot}$ can form in clusters with stellar masses of $\\\\approx10^4$ M$_{\\\\odot}$, and this can increase to well over 1000 M$_{\\\\odot}$ for more massive and denser clusters. The high probability we find for forming a VMS in these mini-haloes at such an early cosmic time makes collisional runaway of Pop. II star clusters a promising channel for producing large numbers of high-redshift IMBHs that may act as the seeds of supermassive black holes.

triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Title and abstract focus on 'runaway stellar collisions' and 'collisional runaway' in star clusters, which is a direct mechanism for producing runaway/high-velocity stars, even though the ultimate goal is black hole formation.

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: Stellar runaway growth in high-redshift nuclear star clusters produces massive VMSs capable of forming IMBH seeds, with implications for early galaxy evolution and metal enrichment via enhanced PISN activity. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework showing that non-uniform Lyman-Werner background radiation from nearby Pop. II star-forming galaxies can significantly suppress molecular hydrogen formation, enabling direct collapse of gaseous cores into massive black hole seeds—offering a viable solution to the problem of how supermassive black holes form at high redshift despite limitations in accretion and radiative feedback. Part II: Direct N-body Simulations: This section describes the setup of direct N-body simulations, including the creation of ellipsoidal star cluster initial conditions with realistic spatial structures, velocities, and densities, and the inclusion of an external dark matter and gas potential with accretion, all evolved using a GPU-accelerated gravity solver. Part I: Cosmological Hydrodynamic Simulations: This section details the setup of cosmological hydrodynamic simulations using RAMSES with a non-equilibrium chemistry solver (KROME) and implements a stringent temperature floor based on the Jeans criterion to prevent numerical fragmentation while accurately resolving star formation conditions. Appendix B Varying the Star Formation Efficiency: Varying the star formation efficiency shows that lower star formation efficiencies lead to smaller stellar masses but higher central densities, enabling the formation of massive stellar clusters (VMSs) despite reduced total mass. Appendix C Alternative Model for Mass Segregation: The paper proposes an alternative mass segregation algorithm based on Šubr et al. (2008) to assess its impact on the formation of massive stars and supernovae in primordial star clusters, finding that it yields results consistent with the original method within statistical errors. Implications for the (early) growth of supermassive black holes: This section argues that runaway stellar collisions in high-redshift, dense, metal-poor star clusters can produce massive stellar remnants (VMSs) capable of seeding supermassive black holes that grow to power high-redshift quasars, with the growth being feasible under Eddington-limited accretion despite uncertainties in post-merger evolution and feedback effects. Appendix A Convergence Tests for Non-Spherical Initial Conditions: Despite increased clump density in higher-resolution non-spherical simulations, the formation fraction of VMSs increases while the average VMS mass remains robust, demonstrating convergence of key outcomes.

title: Seeding High Redshift QSOs by Collisional Runaway in Primordial Star Clusters

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper about runaway stellar collisions in primordial star clusters forming massive stars and black holes. No observational data or catalog of actual stars mentioned.
